content:
# Zechariah — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** Zechariah (Hebrew: Zekharyah, "YHWH Remembers") | **Chapters:** 14

## At a Glance
- **Author:** Zechariah son of Berechiah son of Iddo (1:1), a priest-prophet contemporary with Haggai. However, chapters 9–14 differ so substantially in style, vocabulary, historical setting, and theology that most scholars identify them as a separate composition — "Deutero-Zechariah," dated later (possibly 5th–3rd century BCE). Chapters 9 and 12 each begin with the heading massa ("oracle"), which may mark separate collections.
- **Date:** Chapters 1–8: 520–518 BCE (precisely dated, like Haggai). Chapters 9–14: uncertain, possibly 5th–3rd century BCE.
- **Audience:** Chapters 1–8: the post-exilic community in Jerusalem. Chapters 9–14: uncertain.
- **Genre:** Prophetic vision (chapters 1–6 contain eight night visions — the most elaborate visionary sequence in the prophets), oracle, and proto-apocalyptic literature (chapters 9–14).
- **Timeline:** 520–518 BCE (chapters 1–8); later for chapters 9–14.
- **In One Sentence:** Zechariah moves from eight symbolic night visions encouraging the post-exilic community to a series of increasingly apocalyptic oracles about future judgment and restoration — the most heavily quoted Minor Prophet in the New Testament Passion narratives.

## The World Behind the Text
Chapters 1–8 share the same historical context as Haggai: the returned community in Jerusalem under Darius I, rebuilding the temple. Zechariah is mentioned alongside Haggai in Ezra 5:1, 6:14.

The eight night visions (1:7–6:8) employ an interpreting angel (mal'akh) who explains the visions to the prophet — a literary technique that becomes standard in later apocalyptic literature (Daniel 7–12, Revelation). This development marks a shift: earlier prophets received oracles directly; Zechariah receives visions mediated by angelic interpretation.

Chapters 9–14 reference historical situations (Greece: 9:13 mentions Yavan/Greece; specific geographic locations suggest Hellenistic-period conflicts) that differ from chapters 1–8. The "shepherds" and political imagery may reflect later leadership crises.

## Why This Book Was Written
Chapters 1–8 encourage the rebuilding community. The visions are hopeful: the nations that scattered Judah will be punished (1:18–21), Jerusalem will be inhabited without walls (2:4), Joshua the high priest will be cleansed (chapter 3), Zerubbabel will complete the temple (4:9), and iniquity will be removed from the land (chapter 5).

The "Branch" (tsemach) in 3:8 and 6:12 is a messianic title — "a man whose name is Branch; he will branch out from his place and will build the temple of YHWH." This connects to Isaiah 11:1 (netser, "shoot") and Jeremiah 23:5, 33:15 (tsemach). Whether "Branch" originally referred to Zerubbabel and was later reinterpreted is debated.

Chapters 9–14 shift to eschatological conflict: enemy attacks on Jerusalem, divine intervention, cosmic transformation. The imagery becomes increasingly apocalyptic.

## How to Read This Book
**1:1–6:** Introduction and call to repentance. **1:7–6:8:** The eight night visions (horsemen, horns and craftsmen, measuring line, Joshua the priest, lampstand and olive trees, flying scroll, woman in a basket, four chariots). **6:9–15:** The crowning — Joshua or Zerubbabel? (the text may have been edited; 6:11 crowns Joshua, but 6:12–13 describes "the Branch" who builds the temple, which contextually fits Zerubbabel). **7–8:** Fasting and future feasting. **9–11:** First oracle block — the coming king, the shepherd allegory. **12–14:** Second oracle block — Jerusalem's defense, mourning for "the one they pierced," the final battle.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
Zechariah 9–14 is the most quoted OT source in the Passion narratives:
- 9:9: "Your king comes to you... humble and riding on a donkey" — quoted in Matthew 21:5, John 12:15 (triumphal entry)
- 11:12–13: "Thirty pieces of silver... thrown to the potter" — quoted in Matthew 27:9–10 (Judas's betrayal price; Matthew attributes the quote to Jeremiah, a textual puzzle)
- 12:10: "They will look on me, the one they have pierced" — quoted in John 19:37 (the crucifixion)
- 13:7: "Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered" — quoted in Mark 14:27 / Matthew 26:31 (Jesus' prediction of the disciples' flight)

These quotations mean that Zechariah 9–14, despite being among the most obscure prophetic texts, became central to Christian Passion theology.

Zechariah 14 describes a final battle for Jerusalem, the Mount of Olives splitting in two (14:4), living waters flowing from Jerusalem (14:8), and all nations coming to Jerusalem for the Feast of Tabernacles (14:16). This chapter is among the most apocalyptic in the OT and significantly influenced Revelation.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **The eight night visions (1:7–6:8)** form a structured set with the lampstand vision (chapter 4) at the center. The interpreting angel is an innovation — direct divine speech is mediated through angelic explanation.
- **"Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit" (4:6)** — addressed to Zerubbabel about completing the temple — is one of the most quoted verses from Zechariah, often detached from its specific historical context.
- **The "thirty pieces of silver" in 11:12–13** is the "lordly price" (yoqer) — possibly ironic or sarcastic. The narrative context (the shepherd allegory of chapter 11) is extremely difficult to interpret. Matthew's attribution of this quotation to Jeremiah rather than Zechariah is an unresolved textual question.
- **"The one they pierced" (12:10)** — the Hebrew reads "they will look on me (elai) whom they have pierced," where the first person ("me") suggests the deity speaking. Some Hebrew manuscripts and the Syriac read "on him" (elav). The textual variant affects interpretation significantly.
- **The crowning scene (6:9–15)** may preserve evidence of editorial revision: Joshua is crowned, but the description fits a royal figure. Some scholars suggest Zerubbabel's name was replaced with Joshua's after Zerubbabel's disappearance from the historical record.
- **Dead Sea Scrolls include Zechariah in the Minor Prophets scrolls (4QXIIa,c,e, Mur88).** The text is relatively stable across witnesses.

## Timeline
- **520 BCE:** Zechariah's night visions begin (1:7 — February 519 BCE specifically)
- **518 BCE:** Last dated oracle in chapters 1–8 (7:1)
- **516 BCE:** Second Temple completed
- **5th–3rd century BCE:** Probable date range for chapters 9–14
- **Our manuscripts:** Dead Sea Scrolls (4QXIIa,c,e, Mur88); Leningrad Codex (c. 1008 CE)

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
